Name: Damian Lillard
Fun Fact: He hit one of the most iconic buzzer-beaters in playoff history against OKC in 2019 - pointed at his wrist as if checking the time - then walked off

Name: Russell Westbrook
Fun Fact: He broke Oscar Robertson's 55-year-old triple-double record and didn't stop - he now has nearly 200, a number so absurd the stat feels made up

Name: Jim Otto
Fun Fact: He wore #00 for every one of his 210 consecutive starts - a number the NFL doesn't even officially allow anymore - and made the Hall of Fame doing it
Notes: Number 00 is distinct from 0. Otto defines this number in football as Parish does in basketball.

Name: Robert Parish
Stat Label: Regular Season Games (NBA record at retirement; since passed by LeBron)
Fun Fact: He played 1611 regular season games - more than anyone in NBA history at the time - and won three championships in Boston alongside Bird and McHale. They called him The Chief and he earned every syllable.
Notes: #00 retired by Celtics. Number 00 is distinct from 0.

Name: Oscar Robertson
Stat Label: Season Averaging a Triple-Double (1961-62) — No One Did It Again for 55 Years
Fun Fact: He averaged a triple-double for an entire season in 1961-62 - a feat so absurd it took 55 years for anyone to do it again

Name: Terry Sawchuk
Fun Fact: He recorded 103 shutouts - a record that still stands today - and played through injuries that would have ended anyone else's career

Name: Warren Moon
Fun Fact: He was passed over by every NFL team in 1978 because of his race - spent 6 years dominating the CFL before the NFL finally came calling

Name: Brian Leetch
Fun Fact: He won the Conn Smythe in 1994 as a defenseman - only the third D-man ever to win playoff MVP - and ended the Rangers' 54-year Cup drought

Name: Derek Jeter
Fun Fact: He made The Flip - a relay throw nobody should have been near - to nail Jeremy Giambi at the plate and save a playoff series

Name: Allen Iverson
Fun Fact: He was 6 feet tall and 165 pounds and led the NBA in scoring four times - the Answer proved size didn't matter if your heart was big enough

Name: Babe Ruth
Fun Fact: He was such a dominant pitcher that the Yankees converted him to an outfielder just so he could hit every day - and he became the greatest power hitter who ever lived
Notes: #3 retired by Yankees

Name: Diana Taurasi
Fun Fact: Widely considered the greatest player in WNBA history, she led Phoenix to three championships and became the league's all-time scoring leader

Name: Dwyane Wade
Fun Fact: He carried the Heat back from 2-0 down against Dallas in the 2006 Finals - 34.7 points a night - when the dynasty Mavericks were supposed to win easy

Name: Bobby Orr
Stat Label: Single-Season Assists (1970-71) — D Record, Still Stands
Fun Fact: He set the all-time defenseman record with 102 assists in 1970-71 — and then won the Cup that same spring with the most iconic goal celebration in hockey history, flying through the air with his arms out

Name: Brett Favre
Fun Fact: He started 297 consecutive games at quarterback - the ultimate ironman streak in the NFL - while winning three straight MVP awards in the 1990s
Notes: Defined the gunslinger quarterback archetype

Name: Jean Béliveau
Fun Fact: He won 10 Stanley Cups as a player — more than any player in NHL history — and another seven as a Canadiens executive, 17 total Cup rings on one man's résumé, a record that cannot be broken.
Notes: #4 retired by Canadiens

Name: Lou Gehrig
Fun Fact: He played 2,130 consecutive games - and when ALS finally forced him to stop he stood at home plate and called himself the luckiest man on the face of the earth

Name: Mel Ott
Stat Label: Career Home Runs (First NL Player to 500)
Fun Fact: He was the first National League player to hit 500 home runs — and he did it at the Polo Grounds, a ballpark where the power alleys stretched 447 feet, by turning home plate into his own laboratory with his famous leg-kick swing.
Notes: #4 retired by Giants — played entire career in New York (1926-47)

Name: Paul Molitor
Fun Fact: He had four hits in Game 6 of the 1993 World Series to clinch it for Toronto and won Series MVP as a designated hitter — the only player in history to record 3,000 hits playing primarily as a DH.
Notes: #4 retired by Brewers and Twins

Name: Franz Beckenbauer
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'Der Kaiser', he revolutionized the libero position and captained West Germany to the 1974 World Cup

Name: Joe DiMaggio
Fun Fact: He hit in 56 consecutive games in 1941 - a record so untouchable that statisticians say it's the most unlikely sports achievement in history

Name: Nicklas Lidstrom
Fun Fact: He won seven Norris Trophies as the best defenseman in hockey - a standard of excellence over 20 years that may never be matched

Name: Bill Russell
Fun Fact: The NBA retired his number across all 30 teams in 2022 - 55 years after his last championship - because some legacies take time to fully understand
Notes: SACRED

Name: Stan Musial
Fun Fact: Stan The Man tied Aaron and Mays for most All-Star Games ever played (24) — and he finished with exactly 1,815 hits at home and 1,815 on the road, a symmetry nobody else has ever matched.
Notes: Coexists with SACRED Bill Russell (#6 NBA) — different sport context. Precedent: Rivera coexists at SACRED #42.

Name: Cristiano Ronaldo
Fun Fact: His iconic 'CR7' brand is built around the number he wore at Manchester United, Real Madrid, and with Portugal
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually

Name: David Beckham
Stat Label: England Caps — Most Iconic Player in English Football History
Fun Fact: His last-minute free kick against Greece in 2001 sent England to the World Cup and became one of the most famous goals in English football history

Name: John Elway
Fun Fact: He led 'The Drive' in the 1987 AFC Championship Game - a 98-yard march in the final minutes that became one of the most famous drives in NFL history
Notes: One of the most iconic quarterbacks of the late 20th century

Name: Mickey Mantle
Fun Fact: He hit the longest measured home run in history - 565 feet - at 21 years old - already playing on surgically repaired knees that would have ended anyone else's career before it started

Name: Phil Esposito
Fun Fact: He was the first player in NHL history to score 100 points in a season - in 1968-69 - and he did it so comfortably that everyone knew the game had changed

Name: Alex Ovechkin
Stat Label: Career NHL Goals (All-time record — 926 as of April 2026
Fun Fact: Left Wing
Notes: approved

Name: Cal Ripken Jr.
Fun Fact: He played 2,632 consecutive games - breaking Gehrig's record that everyone said was untouchable - and did it as a shortstop, not a DH

Name: Cale Makar
Fun Fact: He won the Norris, Conn Smythe, and Stanley Cup in the same season at age 23 - one of the greatest seasons a defenseman has ever played
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually CONDITIONAL — cross-generational name test still building. Review annually.

Name: Kobe Bryant
Stat Label: Points in a Single Game (2006) — 2nd Highest in NBA History
Fun Fact: He wore both #8 and #24 - both retired by the Lakers - the only player in NBA history with two retired numbers at the same franchise
Notes: Both 8 and 24 retired by Lakers

Name: Troy Aikman
Fun Fact: Super Bowl XXVII MVP. Won three championships in four years with the Cowboys in the 1990s. Took more hits than any elite QB of his era and kept winning anyway.
Notes: #8 in Cowboys Ring of Honor (not formally retired; Cowboys rarely retire numbers)

Name: Erling Haaland
Stat Label: Premier League Goals in a Season
Fun Fact: He scored 36 Premier League goals in his first season in England - breaking the all-time record by a massive margin
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually CONDITIONAL — cross-generational name test still building. Review annually.

Name: Gordie Howe
Fun Fact: The Gordie Howe Hat Trick is a goal, an assist, and a fight in one game - named for him because he did it so often it became its own category

Name: Maurice Richard
Fun Fact: When the NHL suspended him in 1955, Montreal fans rioted in the streets - the Richard Riot showed what he meant to an entire people

Name: Mia Hamm
Fun Fact: She became the youngest player ever called up to the US Women's National Team at age 15 and helped lead the U.S. to two World Cup titles

Name: Roger Maris
Fun Fact: Broke Babe Ruth’s single-season home run record with 61 in 1961 during one of baseball’s most dramatic seasons

Name: Ronaldo Nazário
Hometown: Bento Ribeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'R9', he returned from devastating knee injuries to lead Brazil to the 2002 World Cup and win the Golden Boot

Name: Ted Williams
Fun Fact: He hit .406 in 1941 and nobody has done it since - and he lost five prime years to two military tours and came back and was still the best hitter alive

Name: Diego Maradona
Fun Fact: In the 1986 World Cup quarterfinal he scored both the 'Hand of God' goal and the 'Goal of the Century' against England within four minutes

Name: Guy Lafleur
Fun Fact: He used to drive to the rink before dawn just to skate alone - that obsession drove five consecutive Stanley Cups

Name: Lionel Messi
Fun Fact: He finally won the 2022 World Cup with Argentina, cementing his place in the debate as the greatest footballer of all time
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually

Name: Marta
Fun Fact: She has won FIFA World Player of the Year six times - more than any player male or female - and carried Brazil to global prominence

Name: Pelé
Fun Fact: He won three World Cups - at 17, 22, and 29 - across three different decades - and scored over 1,000 career goals in professional football

Name: Ronaldinho
Hometown: Porto Alegre, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil
Fun Fact: He once received a standing ovation from Real Madrid fans at the Santiago Bernabéu after scoring two incredible goals for Barcelona

Name: Steve Nash
Fun Fact: He won back-to-back MVPs without a single All-NBA defensive team - proving a point guard could win the award on pure offensive brilliance alone

Name: Zinedine Zidane
Fun Fact: He scored two headers in the 1998 World Cup final to give France its first World Cup title

Name: Isiah Thomas
Fun Fact: In the 1988 Finals he scored 25 points in one quarter on a badly sprained ankle - hobbling up and down the court - one of the great performances in Finals history

Name: Mark Messier
Fun Fact: He guaranteed a win in Game 6 of the 1994 ECF when the Rangers were down 3-2 - and then scored a hat trick - the greatest guarantee in hockey history
Notes: #11 retired by Rangers and Oilers

Name: Mohamed Salah
Fun Fact: He scored 32 Premier League goals in 2017-18 - breaking the record for a 38-game season - and has been Liverpool's best player for seven straight years
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually Active legend — review annually.

Name: Christine Sinclair
Fun Fact: She scored more international goals than any player in soccer history, male or female, and led Canada to Olympic gold in 2021

Name: Joe Namath
Fun Fact: He guaranteed Super Bowl III victory as a 17-point underdog - and then won it - the most famous guarantee in sports history and the moment the AFL became real

Name: John Stockton
Fun Fact: He has 5,000 more career assists than the second-best passer in NBA history - a gap so large it may never be closed

Name: Tom Brady
Fun Fact: He was the 199th pick in the 2000 NFL Draft and went on to win more Super Bowls than any franchise in league history
Notes: #12 retired by Patriots (2024). Not league-wide. Widely considered the greatest QB in NFL history.

Name: Alex Rodriguez
Hometown: New York City, New York, USA
Fun Fact: He became the youngest player in MLB history to reach 500 home runs at age 32
Notes: Legacy complicated by PED suspension but statistical dominance is undeniable

Name: Dan Marino
Fun Fact: He set the single-season passing record in 1984 with 5,084 yards - it stood for 27 years - and he never won a Super Bowl which makes him the greatest what-if in NFL history
Notes: #13 retired by Dolphins

Name: Wilt Chamberlain
Fun Fact: He scored 100 points in a single NBA game in 1962 - a record so far beyond anyone else that it seems fictional - and averaged 50 points per game that entire season

Name: Abby Wambach
Fun Fact: She scored one of the most famous goals in World Cup history with a last-minute header against Brazil in the 2011 quarterfinal

Name: Brendan Shanahan
Fun Fact: He scored 656 goals and dropped the gloves more than almost anyone - a Hall of Famer who combined skill and toughness in a way nobody else did

Name: Johan Cruyff
Fun Fact: His 'Cruyff Turn' move became one of the most famous skills in soccer history and symbolized the Total Football revolution

Name: Pete Rose
Fun Fact: He has 4,256 career hits - 130 more than the second all-time - and he's not in the Hall of Fame because of gambling - the most complicated legacy in baseball
Notes: Gambling ban complicates but doesn't erase the stats

Name: Nikola Jokic
Fun Fact: He's a 6-foot-11 center who makes passes only point guards are supposed to make - three MVPs and a championship make him the most unique dominant player in NBA history
Notes: Active but passes Legend test - per tier list

Name: Thurman Munson
Fun Fact: He died in a plane crash in 1979 at 32 - the Yankees retired his number immediately - the most beloved Yankee of the 1970s dynasty
Notes: #15 retired by Yankees

Name: Joe Montana
Fun Fact: He was 3-for-3 in Super Bowl situations that required a game-winning drive - never threw an interception in four Super Bowls - Cool Joe never flinched

Name: Shohei Ohtani
Stat Label: MVP Awards — Won as Both Pitcher & Hitter (No One Else Has Done This)
Fun Fact: He's the only player in the history of baseball to be an All-Star pitcher and a home run threat at the same time - Ruth is the only comparison and even that falls short
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually Active legend — review annually.

Name: Peyton Manning
Fun Fact: He set the single-season TD record with 55 in 2013 at age 37 - calling audibles at the line with surgical precision - the most cerebral QB who ever played

Name: Johnny Unitas
Fun Fact: He threw a touchdown pass in 47 consecutive games - a record that stood for 52 years - and did it while wearing high-top black cleats that became the symbol of his era

Name: Steve Yzerman
Fun Fact: He waited 11 years to win his first Stanley Cup as Detroit's captain - and when he finally won it he skated around the ice alone with the Cup at 2am
Notes: #19 retired by Red Wings

Name: Tony Gwynn
Fun Fact: In the strike-shortened 1994 season he was hitting .394 when the season stopped - the closest anyone came to .400 since Ted Williams in 1941
Notes: #19 retired by Padres

Name: Barry Sanders
Fun Fact: He retired at his peak with 15,269 rushing yards — 1,457 shy of Walter Payton's then-record — because he didn't want to play for a bad team anymore. The most shocking retirement in NFL history.

Name: Mike Schmidt
Fun Fact: He hit 548 home runs and won 10 Gold Gloves - the most complete third baseman in baseball history - and the Phillies' only World Series title came with him at third

Name: Deion Sanders
Stat Label: Super Bowl Rings (Back-to-Back with Different Teams)
Fun Fact: He is the only person in history to play in both a Super Bowl and a World Series — Braves outfielder, 49ers and Cowboys cornerback — the only athlete who forced quarterbacks to simply not throw to his side of the field.
Notes: Two-sport star — #21 is the definitive modern NFL CB number

Name: Roberto Clemente
Fun Fact: He died on New Year's Eve 1972 on a relief flight to earthquake victims in Nicaragua - the Hall of Fame waived the five-year waiting period and inducted him immediately
Notes: #21 sacred at Pirates

Name: Tim Duncan
Fun Fact: He won five championships spanning three different decades - doing it quietly and fundamentally while everyone else chased flash - the greatest power forward ever

Name: Caitlin Clark
Fun Fact: Her deep-range shooting and playmaking at Iowa helped drive record TV audiences for women's college basketball and made her the NCAA's all-time leading scorer
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually

Name: Emmitt Smith
Fun Fact: He is the all-time NFL rushing leader with 18,355 yards - and won three Super Bowls in the 1990s Cowboys dynasty while doing it

Name: Mike Bossy
Fun Fact: He scored 50 goals in 50 games in the 1980-81 season - matching Rocket Richard's legendary mark - and did it in his last game of that stretch with two goals in the final period
Notes: #22 retired by Islanders

Name: LeBron James
Stat Label: Career Points (NBA All-Time Record — 43440 after 2025-26 season)
Fun Fact: In February 2023 he passed Kareem Abdul-Jabbar as the NBA's all-time leading scorer — a record that had stood for 39 years — and he did it in his twentieth season while still making All-NBA teams.
Notes: Active legend — review annually. Wears #23 with Lakers.

Name: Michael Jordan
Fun Fact: His shot over Bryon Russell to win the 1998 Finals was his final play as a Bull — sixth championship - sixth Finals MVP - and nobody could tell what he'd do next
Notes: #23 retired by Bulls (1994) and Heat (2003, honorary). Not league-wide retired — that's Russell #6 (2022).

Name: Ken Griffey Jr.
Fun Fact: His smooth left-handed swing made him one of the most iconic players of the 1990s and a first-ballot Hall of Famer

Name: Kobe Bryant
Fun Fact: He switched from #8 to #24 midcareer - a reset and rebirth - and both numbers were retired by the Lakers making him the only player in NBA history with two retired numbers at one franchise

Name: Maya Moore
Fun Fact: She stepped away from basketball at her peak to help overturn a wrongful conviction, later marrying the man she helped free

Name: Rickey Henderson
Fun Fact: He stole 1,406 bases - 468 more than the second all-time - and referred to himself in the third person as the greatest of all time which, honestly, he was

Name: Willie Mays
Fun Fact: The Say Hey Kid. Arguably the most complete player in baseball history — elite power

Name: Barry Bonds
Fun Fact: He holds both the single-season record (73 HR, 2001) and the all-time career record (762 HR) - numbers no one else has touched - though the PED cloud over his era means the Hall of Fame door has stayed closed
Notes: PED era - HOF excluded - stats stand. Keep with honest context.

Name: Mark McGwire
Fun Fact: His 1998 chase with Sammy Sosa - both men hitting homers in bunches, the whole country watching - saved baseball after the '94 strike. McGwire later admitted using PEDs. The numbers happened. The debate about what they mean hasn't stopped.
Notes: PED era - admitted user - HOF excluded. Stats real, context required.

Name: John Terry
Stat Label: Premier League Clean Sheets as a Defender (All-Time Record)
Fun Fact: Captain. Leader. Legend. He lifted five Premier League titles wearing #26 — the most by any captain in league history — and scored 41 goals, more than any defender the league has ever seen.

Name: Vladimir Guerrero
Fun Fact: He swung at balls a foot outside the strike zone and hit them for extra bases - the most dangerous bad-ball hitter in baseball history

Name: Marie-Philip Poulin
Fun Fact: She scored the gold medal winning goal in the 2010, 2014, and 2022 Olympics — three different Games, three times Canada needed a goal, three times she delivered. No player in hockey history has matched it.
Notes: CONDITIONAL — greatest women's hockey player; global recognition still building outside Canada. Review annually.

Name: Nathan MacKinnon
Fun Fact: He has won multiple Hart Trophies and a Stanley Cup - widely considered the best player in the NHL - the most complete forward in the game today
Notes: Active but passes Legend test per tier list

Name: Martin Brodeur
Fun Fact: He has 691 career wins - a record so far ahead of everyone else it's essentially untouchable - and won three Stanley Cups doing it

Name: Stephen Curry
Fun Fact: He single-handedly made the three-pointer the centerpiece of basketball offense - four championships and the unanimous MVP with the 73-win season
Notes: Active but passes Legend test per tier list

Name: Carey Price
Fun Fact: In 2014-15 he became the first goaltender in NHL history to win the Hart, Vezina, and Ted Lindsay Awards in a single season — MVP by the writers, best goalie by the GMs, and MVP by his fellow players all at once.

Name: Greg Maddux
Stat Label: Consecutive Cy Young Awards (1992–1995 — Only Pitcher Ever)
Fun Fact: He won four straight Cy Youngs throwing 78 mph when he could throw 92 — outsmarting lineups so completely that hitters said facing him felt like taking a final exam they hadn't studied for. The Professor earned every syllable.
Notes: #31 retired by Cubs and Braves. 355 career wins (8th all-time). 3371 strikeouts.

Name: Magic Johnson
Fun Fact: He played center in the deciding game of his rookie Finals - a 6-foot-9 point guard starting at center and scoring 42 points - the performance that announced he was different
Notes: #32 sacred at Lakers

Name: Sandy Koufax
Fun Fact: From 1963-1966 he was the most dominant pitcher in baseball history - four no-hitters including a perfect game - then he retired at 30 because his arm gave out
Notes: #32 sacred at Dodgers

Name: Jose Canseco
Fun Fact: In 1988 he became the first player in MLB history to record 40 home runs and 40 stolen bases in a single season

Name: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar
Fun Fact: His skyhook was the most unstoppable shot in basketball history — he scored 38,387 points with it without anyone figuring out how to guard it, a record that stood for 39 years until LeBron passed him in 2023.
Notes: #33 retired by Bucks and Lakers (and Power Memorial HS)

Name: Larry Bird
Fun Fact: He once told the other team exactly where he was going to shoot from and then did it - three MVPs and three championships next door to Magic's 32
Notes: #33 sacred at Celtics

Name: Patrick Ewing
Fun Fact: He carried the Knicks on his back for 15 seasons and never won a title - the greatest player to never win a ring alongside Barkley and Stockton
Notes: #33 retired by Knicks

Name: Patrick Roy
Fun Fact: He used to talk to his goalposts before games and demand a trade mid-game from the Montreal bench in front of 20,000 people - the most magnetic personality in hockey history
Notes: #33 retired by Canadiens and Avalanche

Name: Bo Jackson
Stat Label: All-Star Appearances in Two Major Sports
Fun Fact: He is the only athlete ever named an All-Star in two major sports — MLB in 1989, NFL in 1990 — before a hip injury ended both careers at age 28, after which the myth of what could have been only grew.
Notes: Wore #16 with Royals — #34 is the football icon number

Name: Charles Barkley
Fun Fact: Despite being undersized for a power forward he dominated the NBA in the 1990s and won MVP in 1993 leading the Suns to the Finals

Name: David Ortiz
Fun Fact: One of the greatest postseason hitters ever, he led the Red Sox to three World Series titles and delivered one of the most famous grand slams in playoff history in the 2013 ALCS

Name: Giannis Antetokounmpo
Fun Fact: Delivered one of the greatest Finals performances ever with 50 points to clinch the Bucks’ first championship in 50 years

Name: Hakeem Olajuwon
Fun Fact: In 1994 he became the only player in NBA history to win MVP, Defensive Player of the Year, and Finals MVP in the same season — then he won it all again the next year, back-to-back titles in the only two years Michael Jordan stepped away from basketball.
Notes: #34 retired by Rockets

Name: Paul Pierce
Fun Fact: The Truth. Draped in the Celtics' #34 for 15 seasons — he delivered the 2008 championship and earned Finals MVP. One of the most clutch shot-makers of his era.
Notes: #34 retired by Celtics

Name: Shaquille O'Neal
Fun Fact: He was so physically dominant in 2000-2002 that referees had to create new rules just to slow him down - three consecutive Finals MVPs

Name: Walter Payton
Fun Fact: Sweetness - he ran with the grace of a dancer and the force of a freight train - the most complete running back in NFL history
Notes: #34 sacred at Bears

Name: Kevin Durant
Fun Fact: He named his number 35 in honor of his AAU coach Charles Craig, who was murdered at age 35 and helped shape Durant's career
Notes: One of the greatest pure scorers in NBA history

Name: Dominik Hašek
Fun Fact: Nicknamed 'The Dominator', he won back-to-back NHL MVP awards in 1997 and 1998 — an almost unheard-of feat for a goaltender
Notes: #39 retired by Sabres. Not league-wide.

Name: Gale Sayers
Fun Fact: He was inducted into the Hall of Fame at 34 - the youngest ever - after injuries cut his career short - Brian's Song made him known to a generation beyond football
Notes: #40 sacred at Bears

Name: Dirk Nowitzki
Stat Label: Career Points — 21 Seasons, One Team, One City
Fun Fact: He won the 2011 Finals MVP on a team that wasn't supposed to beat LeBron's Heat - the only player to win Finals MVP against a team that was favored by everyone - and went back to Dallas every single season

Name: Tom Seaver
Fun Fact: He was so good the Mets named him Tom Terrific and built a championship around him - three Cy Youngs and the 1969 Miracle Mets

Name: Jackie Robinson
Fun Fact: His number is retired across all of baseball - not one team, all of them - and every April 15th every player in baseball wears 42 because what he did was bigger than baseball
Notes: SACRED - retired across all MLB

Name: Mariano Rivera
Fun Fact: His cut fastball was so dominant that hitters knew it was coming and still couldn't hit it - he became the first player ever unanimously elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Notes: Greatest closer in baseball history; #42 already appears as SACRED for Jackie Robinson but Rivera is the defining player of the number in the modern era

Name: Ronnie Lott
Fun Fact: Rather than miss a playoff game in 1985 he had part of his left pinky amputated — the most intimidating safety in NFL history and one of the ten greatest defenders ever to play the game.
Notes: Coexists with Jackie Robinson SACRED #42 — 49ers-era CB/S icon

Name: Troy Polamalu
Fun Fact: He played with long flowing hair that flew behind him when he blitzed - a supernatural force at safety who seemed to know where the ball was going before the snap

Name: Hank Aaron
Fun Fact: He received death threats while chasing Babe Ruth's home run record in 1973 - and kept playing - and broke it on April 8, 1974 in front of a roaring crowd in Atlanta
Notes: #44 retired by Braves

Name: Jerry West
Fun Fact: His silhouette is the NBA logo — and he's the only player to win Finals MVP on the losing team (1969) — a title the league quietly retired afterward because no one should have to win it that way again

Name: Reggie Jackson
Fun Fact: Mr. October. Hit three home runs on three consecutive pitches in Game 6 of the 1977 World Series — possibly the most dominant single-game performance in postseason history.
Notes: #44 retired by Yankees; wore #9 in Oakland (retired 2004 as #9)

Name: Pedro Martinez
Fun Fact: In 1999 and 2000 he was so far ahead of his era that his statistics look like a video game - the best two-year pitching run of the modern era

Name: David Robinson
Stat Label: Points Scored on Final Day of Season to Win the 1994 Scoring Title
Fun Fact: He gave up two prime seasons to serve his Navy commitment before his NBA career - then led the league in scoring, won MVP, and brought Tim Duncan to San Antonio

Name: Mike Singletary
Fun Fact: Samurai Mike — his eyes seemed to glow on film. In the 46 defense that destroyed the 1985 NFL he called the checks, delivered the hits, and tied a Super Bowl record with two fumble recoveries in SB XX.

Name: Bernie Williams
Fun Fact: The quiet engine of the Yankees dynasty. Four championships, a batting title, Gold Glove defense, and a .298 career average. Also a classically trained guitarist — released two albums after baseball.
Notes: #51 retired by Yankees

Name: Dick Butkus
Fun Fact: He defined the position. Coaches taught kids to play middle linebacker by showing them Butkus film — 8 Pro Bowls in 9 seasons, 22 interceptions, 27 fumble recoveries, and a menace that every MLB since has been measured against.
Notes: #51 retired by Bears 1994.

Name: Ichiro Suzuki
Stat Label: Hits in a Single Season (2004 MLB Record)
Fun Fact: He arrived in MLB at 27 and immediately broke the single-season hits record. Ten Gold Gloves. A .311 career average. Approached the game like a craftsman — same routine every single day for 28 years.
Notes: #51 retired by Mariners

Name: Randy Johnson
Fun Fact: The Big Unit was 6-foot-10 and threw 100mph - facing him was described by hitters as looking up at a skyscraper throwing lightning

Name: Ray Lewis
Stat Label: Defensive Player of the Year Awards
Fun Fact: He danced before every game in a way that made opposing teams genuinely afraid - he won Super Bowl MVP in his first ring, and came back 17 years into his career to win a second
Notes: #52 sacred at Ravens

Name: Brian Urlacher
Stat Label: Pro Bowl Selections + 2005 Defensive Player of the Year
Fun Fact: He was the quarterback of the Bears defense for 13 years - the fastest linebacker in the NFL who could cover wide receivers and lay out running backs

Name: Junior Seau
Fun Fact: He played 20 seasons with relentless ferocity and died in 2012 - his CTE diagnosis helped change how the NFL treats player safety

Name: Lawrence Taylor
Fun Fact: He won NFL MVP in 1986 as a defensive player - something that almost never happens - and offensive tackles literally changed how they played because of him
Notes: Widely considered the greatest defensive player in NFL history

Name: Jack Lambert
Fun Fact: He played without his two front teeth and once said quarterbacks should wear dresses because of how protected they were becoming
Notes: The face of the Steelers' Steel Curtain defense

Name: Chuck Bednarik
Stat Label: Two-Way All-Pro Selections (Offense & Defense, Same Season) — Last Player Ever
Fun Fact: He was the last two-way player in NFL history - played 60 full minutes of center and linebacker - Concrete Charlie was a different species

Name: Mario Lemieux
Fun Fact: He survived cancer mid-career, came back and scored a goal in his first game back, and then won a second Stanley Cup - the most remarkable comeback in hockey history
Notes: #66 retired by Penguins - played on same line as Jagr #68

Name: Jaromir Jagr
Fun Fact: He chose #68 to honor the Prague Spring of 1968 when Soviet tanks rolled into Czechoslovakia - every time he put on the jersey it meant something political and personal
Notes: Adjacent to Lemieux #66 - two linemates, two Legends

Name: Evgeni Malkin
Fun Fact: In 2009 he became the first Russian to win the Conn Smythe, adding an Art Ross and his first Cup along the way — then in 2012 he won the Hart and another Art Ross as regular-season MVP. Three Cups total. Twenty seasons playing alongside Crosby probably cost him half his legacy.
Notes: Active legend — review annually

Name: Mean Joe Greene
Fun Fact: The Coca-Cola ad where he gives a kid his jersey made him the most famous defensive player in America - four Super Bowls and the face of the Steel Curtain

Name: P.K. Subban
Stat Label: Norris Trophy (2013) — Best Defenseman in the NHL
Fun Fact: He was the loudest, most electric personality in a sport that spent decades rewarding quiet - donated $10 million to a children's hospital - and played defense like it was supposed to be entertaining
Notes: CONDITIONAL — not yet HOF-inducted. Cross-generational name test building. Review at induction.

Name: Ray Bourque
Fun Fact: He gave up his #7 so Phil Esposito could retire it - wore #77 for the rest of his career - and the whole league cheered when he finally won the Cup in Colorado at 40
Notes: #77 retired by Bruins

Name: Anthony Muñoz
Stat Label: Pro Bowl Selections (Most Ever at OT)
Fun Fact: He went to 11 straight Pro Bowls and was named first-team All-Pro nine times — widely considered the greatest offensive tackle in NFL history and the only Bengal ever inducted as a unanimous first-ballot Hall of Famer.
Notes: #78 retired by Bengals

Name: Jerry Rice
Fun Fact: NFL.com named him the greatest player in NFL history - not the greatest receiver - the greatest player, period - and it's hard to argue

Name: Terrell Owens
Fun Fact: He did situps in his driveway during a public holdout and made it appointment television - one of the most explosive and controversial players in NFL history

Name: Randy Moss
Fun Fact: He ran past defenders so easily in his prime that it looked like they were standing still - the most physically gifted receiver in NFL history
Notes: Note: wore #84 in Minnesota, #81 in New England

Name: Jack Hughes
Fun Fact: He scored the golden goal against Canada at the 2026 Winter Olympics — one of the most electric moments in USA hockey history, in one of sport's greatest rivalries
Notes: CONDITIONAL — Olympic golden goal clears permanence and global floor tests. Cross-generational name test still building. Review July 2026. Verify exact game details.

Name: Rob Gronkowski
Fun Fact: In 2011 at age 22 he set the single-season TD record for tight ends with 17 — then came out of retirement seven years later to add a fourth Super Bowl ring in Tampa. The most physically dominant tight end ever.
Notes: First-ballot HOF eligible 2026

Name: Sidney Crosby
Fun Fact: He shot pucks at a dryer in his basement as a kid until the dryer broke - the work ethic behind three Stanley Cups and three Hart Trophies
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually Active legend — review annually.

Name: Travis Kelce
Fun Fact: He has three Super Bowl rings and in 2025 passed Jerry Rice for the most receptions in Super Bowl history with 35 — the most reliable weapon any quarterback has ever had.
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually - may move to Legend on retirement

Name: Eric Lindros
Fun Fact: He was the most physically dominant player in the NHL in the mid-90s - 6-foot-4 and could skate like a wing - concussions cut short what could have been the greatest career ever

Name: Patrick Kane
Fun Fact: He scored the overtime Cup winner in 2010 - a goal so fast the arena didn't know it was in for 30 seconds - and then won two more in the most dominant dynasty in modern NHL history
Notes: CONDITIONAL — cross-generational name test building. ACTIVE - review annually.

Name: Tony Gonzalez
Fun Fact: He has 1,325 career receptions - the most by any tight end in NFL history - and redefined what the position could be
Notes: #88 is most HOF-crowded number in NFL history per tier list

Name: Mike Ditka
Fun Fact: He was the first tight end inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame - revolutionized the position - and then coached the Bears to the greatest season in NFL history in 1985

Name: Sergei Fedorov
Fun Fact: He became the first Russian player to win the NHL Hart Trophy in 1994 and was known for being able to dominate both offensively and defensively
Notes: One of the greatest two-way forwards in NHL history

Name: Michael Strahan
Fun Fact: He set the single-season sack record with 22.5 in 2001 - and helped the Giants beat the undefeated Patriots in Super Bowl XLII - one of the greatest upsets in NFL history

Name: Reggie White
Fun Fact: The Minister of Defense - 198 career sacks - a Hall of Famer who combined uncommon physical gifts with genuine religious conviction that he wore openly

Name: John Randle
Fun Fact: Undrafted out of Texas A&M-Kingsville, he took the field in eye-black war paint and relentless trash talk — and posted 137.5 sacks, the most ever by a defensive tackle at the time of his retirement.

Name: Pavel Bure
Fun Fact: He scored 60 goals in back-to-back seasons in 1993-94 and 1994-95 - the Russian Rocket was the most electrifying player of his era

Name: Connor McDavid
Fun Fact: He skates faster than anyone who has ever played the game - multiple Hart Trophies and Art Ross Trophies - the best player in hockey right now without debate
Notes: ACTIVE - review annually Active legend — review annually.

Name: Wayne Gretzky
Fun Fact: If you remove every goal he ever scored he would still be the all-time points leader just from his assists - #99 was retired across the entire NHL in 1999
Notes: SACRED - retired across all NHL

Name: Aaron Judge
Fun Fact: In 2022 he hit 62 home runs — breaking Roger Maris's AL record that had stood since 1961 — without a single PED allegation. The cleanest power season of the modern era.
Notes: Active legend — review annually

Name: Ed Reed
Fun Fact: He had 64 career interceptions and returned them for more yards than any safety in NFL history — he didn't just pick the ball off, he punished you for throwing it.

Name: Joe Morgan
Fun Fact: He was the engine of the Big Red Machine — back-to-back MVP, back-to-back World Series, five straight Gold Gloves. The 5'7" second baseman who was better at almost everything than almost everyone.
Notes: #8 retired by Cincinnati Reds in 1998.

Name: Tony Dorsett
Fun Fact: In a Monday Night game against Minnesota, he took a handoff from his own 1-yard line with only 10 men on the field — a miscommunication — and ran 99 yards for a touchdown. Still the longest run from scrimmage in NFL history.

Name: Jeremy Roenick
Fun Fact: One of only six American-born players to score 500+ NHL goals — he played with a recklessness and a mouth that made him impossible to ignore. The face of American hockey before anyone thought that was possible.
Notes: HOF 2024. Wore #27 in Chicago; switched to #97 in Phoenix.

Name: Tim Horton
Fun Fact: Teammates said he was the strongest man in hockey — could bend steel bars with his hands. He died in a car crash at 44 in 1974. His business partner kept a little donut chain going in his name. Tim Hortons now has over 5,000 locations.
Notes: #7 retired by Toronto Maple Leafs in 2016 (shared with King Clancy).

Name: Dave Keon
Stat Label: Conn Smythe Trophy (only Maple Leaf to ever win it)
Fun Fact: He won the 1967 Conn Smythe Trophy with just 8 playoff points — because his job wasn't to score, it was to make Jean Béliveau disappear. He's the only Maple Leaf to ever win it.
Notes: #14 retired by Toronto Maple Leafs.

Name: Kirby Puckett
Fun Fact: Before Game 6 of the 1991 World Series, he told his teammates 'jump on my back.' He then robbed Ron Gant with a leaping catch at the wall, and hit a walk-off homer in the 11th inning to force Game 7. The Twins won it all the next night.
Notes: #34 retired by Minnesota Twins in 1997. Career ended at 35 due to sudden onset glaucoma.

Name: Paolo Maldini
Fun Fact: He wore #3 for AC Milan for 25 years and never played for another club. When he retired in 2009, Milan retired the number with him — unless one of his sons ever plays for the club. That's how you exit.
Notes: #3 retired by AC Milan.

Name: Roberto Carlos
Fun Fact: In 1997, he struck a free kick that physicists said was impossible — it swerved wide of the wall, then curved back into the net. The French goalkeeper just watched it go in. It remains the most analyzed goal in the history of the sport.

Name: Bobby Moore
Stat Label: FIFA World Cup Title (1966, as captain)
Fun Fact: After England won the 1966 World Cup, Pelé called Moore the greatest defender he'd ever faced. He played the whole thing without committing a single foul. That was the superpower — reading the game so perfectly you never needed to tackle.
Notes: #6 retired by West Ham United.

Name: Xavi
Fun Fact: He played 767 games for Barcelona wearing #6 — every single one a clinic in how to control a football match without the ball ever looking like it was in danger. Tiki-taka wasn't a system. It was just Xavi.

Name: Franco Baresi
Fun Fact: He played the 1994 World Cup Final just six weeks after knee surgery. Italy lost on penalties and he missed the first kick. He's still regarded as the greatest libero in the history of the game. Some exits are earned that way.
Notes: #6 retired by AC Milan.

Name: George Best
Fun Fact: They called him El Beatle. He wore #7 when it mattered most — including the 1968 European Cup Final, where he dribbled past the goalkeeper and rolled the ball in as casually as a training session. Won the Ballon d'Or that same year.
Notes: Numbers were positional in Best's era; wore #7 in 141 matches including the 1968 European Cup Final.

Name: Thierry Henry
Fun Fact: He scored 30 goals in the 2003-04 season — the year Arsenal went the entire Premier League unbeaten. Ask any Arsenal fan what #14 means. There's only one right answer.

Name: Bob Gibson
Fun Fact: In 1968 he posted a 1.12 ERA — the lowest in modern baseball history. The league was so dominated by pitching that year that MLB literally lowered the mound the following season. They lowered the mound because of Bob Gibson.
Notes: #45 retired by St. Louis Cardinals in 1975.
